papers.title: Spectroscopic Follow-Up of Candidate Runaway Stars
papers.author_names: GUILLERMO TORRES RALPH NEUHÄUSER SEBASTIAN A. HütTEL VALERI V. HAMBARYAN

abstract.text: Runaway stars are characterized by higher space velocities than typical field stars. They are presumed to have been ejected from their birth places by one or more energetic mechanisms, including supernova explosions. Accurate radial velocities are essential for investigating their origin, by tracing back their Galactic orbits to look for close encounters in space and in time with neutron stars and young associations. While most studies of runaways have focused on OB stars, later-type stars have also been considered on occasion. Here we report the results of a long-term high-resolution spectroscopic monitoring program with the goal of providing accurate radial velocities for 188 runaway candidates of spectral type A and later, proposed by Tetzlaff et al. (2011). We obtained multiple measurements over a period of about 13 yr to guard against the possibility that some may be members of binary or multiple systems, adding archival observations going back another 25 yr in some cases. We report new spectroscopic orbital solutions for more than three dozen systems. Many more are also found to be binaries based on available astrometric information. A small-scale study carried out here to trace back the paths of our targets together with those of four well-studied, optically-visible neutron stars among the so-called Magnificent Seven, resulted in no credible encounters.

brief.tldr: [observational astrophysics research paper] A long-term spectroscopic monitoring campaign of 188 A- and later-type runaway star candidates reveals over 30 spectroscopic binaries and multiple systems, with precise radial velocity solutions derived from ~13 years of observations. The study demonstrates that Gaia's astrometric data alone are insufficient to detect or resolve binarity, leading to biased velocity measurements; new orbital solutions confirm binarity in many cases previously flagged only by Gaia. A pilot traceback analysis of target orbits with known neutron stars finds no credible past encounters, placing constraints on the supernova ejection scenario for these late-type runaways.

source_fields.title: Spectroscopic Follow-Up of Candidate Runaway Stars

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: The Sample: The sample of 188 runaway star candidates was selected from Tetzlaff et al. (2011) based on high runaway probability or youth, with kinematic and astrometric data from Gaia DR3 and additional information from SIMBAD to support spectroscopic follow-up. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to identify and date runaway stars—particularly late-type stars—by leveraging lithium depletion and stellar evolution models to overcome the limitations of traditional age-determination techniques, thereby enabling more accurate tracing of ejection events beyond the typical 30 Myr timeframe and expanding the scope of runaway star studies beyond OB stars. Concluding Remarks: This section summarizes a decade-long spectroscopic and astrometric study that identifies and characterizes binary and multiple systems among A- and later-type runaway stars, enabling more accurate space velocity measurements to investigate their origins and potential supernova progenitor histories. Binaries In The Sample: This section details the identification and analysis of binary and multiple star systems in the sample, emphasizing that mean velocities in Table 4 are often center-of-mass velocities derived from orbital solutions and should be used for Galactic orbit calculations, while noting uncertainties and limitations due to spectroscopic binaries, long-period variability, and pulsational effects. Spectroscopic Observations: Spectroscopic observations of the targets were primarily conducted using the TRES spectrograph on the Tillinghast reflector, with archival data from similar instruments at Oak Ridge, providing high-resolution spectra used for radial velocity measurements and astrophysical analysis. Peculiar Velocities And Runaway Status: Most stars in the sample exhibit peculiar velocities exceeding the 30 km s⁻¹ runaway threshold, confirming the effectiveness of earlier runaway candidate selections, while six stars—two of which are long-period single-lined spectroscopic binaries at large distances—reach velocities over 100 km s⁻¹. Traceback Of Runaway Stars: A Pilot Study: This section presents a Monte Carlo traceback analysis of runaway stars and four nearby neutron stars (the Magnificent Seven) to assess past close encounters with young associations or neutron stars, finding no credible ejection events within 1 pc, highlighting uncertainties in distances and radial velocities as dominant factors.

papers.title: The impact of the Galactic bar and the Large Magellanic Cloud on hypervelocity star trajectories
papers.author_names: Isabella Armstrong Fraser A. Evans Jo Bovy

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Galactic Center (GC) at speeds faster than the Galactic escape velocity are useful tools to provide insight into the Milky Way's dark matter halo. However, most characterizations of HVS orbits assume static models of the Milky Way's gravitational potential. In this work, we assess the influence of the Galactic bar and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) on HVS trajectories, comparing them with those from an axisymmetric potential. We simulate 28,000 HVSs ejected over the last 100 Myr and find that ignoring the bar and LMC can cause their apparent ejection location to drift by up to 100 pc. Applying two standard HVS potential fitting methods to our sample shows that they are unable to perform as designed when non-axisymmetric effects are neglected. We calculate the angle between HVS Galactocentric position and velocity and find the LMC and bar can induce a deflection angle of up to several degrees. Using mock Gaia Data Release 4 observations, however, we show that this deflection is too small in magnitude to be measured in the near future without significantly improved observational uncertainties, particularly in heliocentric distance. Our results emphasize the need to account for the bar and LMC in modeling the Galactic potential using HVSs as a tracer.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Numerical simulations of 28,000 hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Galactic Center over the last 100 Myr reveal that the Galactic bar and Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) induce significant non-axisymmetric deflections in HVS trajectories—up to 100 pc in apparent ejection location and several degrees in position-velocity angle—compromising standard axisymmetric potential fitting methods. Despite these effects, mock Gaia DR4 observations show the deflections are too small to detect with current observational uncertainties, especially in heliocentric distance, highlighting the need for non-axisymmetric potential models in HVS-based dynamical modeling.

source_fields.title: The impact of the Galactic bar and the Large Magellanic Cloud on hypervelocity star trajectories

source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes a method that propagates synthetic high-velocity star (HVS) trajectories forward and backward in time through five different Milky Way gravitational potentials—ranging from axisymmetric to fully non-axisymmetric models including the bar, Large Magellanic Cloud, and Galactic center reflex motion—to quantify how these potential components affect HVS evolution and dynamics. Results: The inclusion of the bar, LMC, and reflex motion in the Galactic potential causes HVSs to miss the Galactic Center by up to several hundred parsecs when back-propagated in a MW-only potential, with significant angular momentum buildup—especially in MW+All—highlighting the critical need for non-axisymmetric and time-dependent components in HVS origin studies. Conclusion: Non-axisymmetric components of the Milky Way's gravitational potential, such as the Galactic bar and the LMC with its reflex motion, significantly affect hypervelocity star trajectories and must be included in potential reconstruction models to avoid systematic errors. Discussion: All four non-axisymmetric potentials (MW+Bar, MW+LMC, MW+NIF, MW+All) significantly distort HVS trajectories compared to axisymmetric models, rendering existing potential-constraining methods (C19 and G22) ineffective, with deviations in velocity and mass distributions influenced by uncertainties in Galactic potential parameters such as bar strength, LMC mass, and ejection mechanisms. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel dynamical modeling approach that leverages the known past location of hypervelocity stars ejected from the Galactic Center to break degeneracies in Milky Way potential inference, providing a more robust constraint on the gravitational potential by utilizing their unique two-phase-space measurements.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Galactic Center (GC) at speeds faster than the Galactic escape velocity are useful tools to provide insight into the Milky Way's dark matter halo.
title: The impact of the Galactic bar and the Large Magellanic Cloud on hypervelocity star trajectories

papers.title: The role of Massive Black Holes in merging star clusters: dynamical evolution, stellar & compact object ejections and gravitational waves
papers.author_names: Lazaros Souvaitzis Antti Rantala Thorsten Naab

abstract.text: Star clusters can interact and merge in galactic discs, halos, or centers. We present direct N-body simulations of binary mergers of star clusters with $M_{\\\\star} = 2.7 \\\\times 10^4 \\\\: \\\\mathrm{M_{\\\\odot}}$ each, using the N-body code BIFROST with subsystem regularisation and post-Newtonian dynamics. We include 500 $\\\\mathrm{M_{\\\\odot}}$ massive black holes (MBHs) in the progenitors to investigate their impact on remnant evolution. The MBHs form hard binaries interacting with stars and stellar black holes (BHs). A few Myr after the cluster merger, this produces sizable populations of runaway stars ($\\\\sim$800 with $v_{\\\\mathrm{ej}} \\\\gtrsim 50 \\\\mathrm{kms^{-1}}$) and stellar BHs ($\\\\sim$30) escaping within 100 Myr. The remnants lose $\\\\sim30\\\\%$ of their BH population and $\\\\sim3\\\\%$ of their stars, with $\\\\sim$30 stars accelerated to high velocities $\\\\gtrsim 300 \\\\mathrm{kms^{-1}}$. Comparison simulations of isolated clusters with central hard MBH binaries and cluster mergers without MBHs show that the process is driven by MBH binaries, while those with a single 1000 $\\\\mathrm{M_{\\\\odot}}$ MBH in isolated or merging clusters produce fewer runaway stars at lower velocities. Low-eccentricity merger orbits yield rotating remnants ($v_{\\\\mathrm{rot}} \\\\sim 3 \\\\mathrm{kms^{-1}}$) , but probing the presence of MBHs via kinematics alone remains challenging. We expect the binary MBHs to merge within a Hubble time, producing observable gravitational-wave (GW) events detectable by future GW detectors such as the Einstein Telescope and LISA. The results suggest that interactions with low-mass MBH binaries formed in merging star clusters are an important additional channel for producing runaway and high-velocity stars, free-floating stellar BHs and compact objects.

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes a series of direct N-body simulations using the GPU-accelerated BIFROST code to study how massive black holes influence stellar cluster mergers, focusing on the core idea that MBHs enhance the ejection of stars and compact objects via dynamical interactions in the merger remnant. Conclusion: The presence of massive black holes in merging star clusters significantly enhances the production of high-velocity escaping stars and compact objects, with MBHs playing a crucial role in driving dynamical ejecta and altering the cluster's evolutionary outcome, though having minimal observable impact on the kinematic structure of the merger remnants. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that links the dynamical ejection of hyper-velocity stars and runaway stars to interactions between star clusters and massive black holes, addressing the unresolved problem of the origin of high-velocity stellar populations by unifying multiple ejection mechanisms within a coherent cluster merger and dynamical evolution model. Properties Of The Star Clusters: The presence of single or binary massive black holes in merged star clusters drives rapid core expansion and mass loss through dynamical interactions, leading to flatter central density profiles via core scouring, with the merger and evolution timescales depending on the number and eccentricity of the black holes. The Ejection Of Stars And Compact Objects: This section classifies ejected stars and compact objects based on their ejection velocities, linking each category—low-velocity, runaway, hyper-runaway, hyper-velocity, and extreme-velocity—to specific dynamical mechanisms, and shows that the presence of massive black hole binaries significantly increases the number and rate of ejections compared to simulations without them. Formation And Evolution Of Massive Black Hole Binaries: The formation and evolution of massive black hole binaries in stellar clusters are governed by dynamical interactions that drive their hardening and eccentricity growth, with the binary's semi-major axis shrinking due to energy loss from stellar encounters and reaching a "hard" phase when the separation falls below a critical value defined by the local velocity dispersion and mass density. Dynamical Ejection Mechanisms (Dem) In Star & Globular Clusters: Dynamical ejection mechanisms in star and globular clusters, including single-star encounters and interactions with binary systems, can produce high-velocity stars that serve as observational signatures of massive black hole seeds or stellar-mass black hole populations in cluster cores.
